But the third example shows a counterexample.

          have h₅₈ : ∀ (n : ℕ) (primes : List ℕ), n = (countFactors n primes).foldl (fun acc (p, e) => acc * p ^ e) 1 := by
            intro n primes
            induction' primes with p ps ih
            · simp [countFactors]
            · simp_all [countFactors, List.foldl]
              <;>
              (try cases n <;> simp_all [countFactor, Nat.div_eq_of_lt, Nat.mod_eq_of_lt, Nat.Prime.one_lt]) <;>
              (try {
                have h₅₉ := Nat.Prime.one_lt (by aesop : Nat.Prime p)
                have h₆₀ : n % p = 0 → n / p < n := by
                  intro h₆₁
                  have h₆₂ : p > 1 := by linarith
                  have h₆₃ : n / p < n := Nat.div_lt_self (by omega) h₆₂
                  exact h₆₃
                by_cases h₆₄ : n % p = 0
                · simp_all [h₆₄]
                  <;>
                  (try omega) <;>
                  (try ring_nf at * <;> simp_all [Nat.pow_succ])
                · simp_all [h₆₄]
                  <;>
                  (try omega) <;>
                  (try ring_nf at * <;> simp_all [Nat.pow_succ])
              }) <;>
              (try {
                simp_all [countFactor, Nat.div_eq_of_lt, Nat.mod_eq_of_lt, Nat.Prime.one_lt]
                <;>
                (try omega) <;>
                (try ring_nf at * <;> simp_all [Nat.pow_succ])
              }) <;>
              (try {
                have h₅₉ := Nat.Prime.one_lt (by aesop : Nat.Prime p)
                have h₆₀ : n % p = 0 → n / p < n := by
                  intro h₆₁
                  have h₆₂ : p > 1 := by linarith
                  have h₆₃ : n / p < n := Nat.div_lt_self (by omega) h₆₂
                  exact h₆₃
                by_cases h₆₄ : n % p = 0
                · simp_all [h₆₄]
                  <;>
                  (try omega) <;>
                  (try ring_nf at * <;> simp_all [Nat.pow_succ])
                · simp_all [h₆₄]
                  <;>
                  (try omega) <;>
                  (try ring_nf at * <;> simp_all [Nat.pow_succ])
              })
          exact h₅₈ n primes
        -- Since countFactors is the same as findExponents, we can use the above result
        have h₅₉ : countFactors n primes = findExponents n primes ‹_› := by rfl
        rw [h₅₉] at h₅₇
        exact h₅₇
      -- Apply the general result to our specific case
      have h₆₀ : n = (findExponents n primes h_precond).foldl (fun acc (p, e) => acc * p ^ e) 1 := by
        apply h₅₂
        <;> assumption
      exact h₆₀
    exact h₅₁
  have h₆ : (findExponents n primes h_precond).all (fun (p, _) => p ∈ primes) := by
    have h₆₁ : (findExponents n primes h_precond).all (fun (p, _) => p ∈ primes) := by
      -- Prove that every prime in the result is in the original list of primes
      have h₆₂ : ∀ (n : ℕ) (primes : List ℕ), findExponents_precond n primes → (findExponents n primes ‹_›).all (fun (p, _) => p ∈ primes) := by
        intro n primes h
        have h₆₃ : n > 0 := h.1
        have h₆₄ : primes.length > 0 := h.2.1
        have h₆₅ : primes.all (fun p => Nat.Prime p) := h.2.2.1
        have h₆₆ : List.Nodup primes := h.2.2.2
        -- Use the fact that the countFactors function only uses primes from the original list
        have h₆₇ : (countFactors n primes).all (fun (p, _) => p ∈ primes) := by
          have h₆₈ : ∀ (n : ℕ) (primes : List ℕ), (countFactors n primes).all (fun (p, _) => p ∈ primes) := by
            intro n primes
            induction' primes with p ps ih
            · simp [countFactors]
            · simp_all [countFactors, List.all_cons]
              <;>
              (try aesop) <;>
              (try {
                aesop
              })
          exact h₆₈ n primes
        -- Since countFactors is the same as findExponents, we can use the above result
        have h₆₉ : countFactors n primes = findExponents n primes ‹_› := by rfl
        rw [h₆₉] at h₆₇
        exact h₆₇
      -- Apply the general result to our specific case
      have h₇₀ : (findExponents n primes h_precond).all (fun (p, _) => p ∈ primes) := by
        apply h₆₂
        <;> assumption
      exact h₇₀
    exact h₆₁
  have h₇ : primes.all (fun p => (findExponents n primes h_precond).any (fun pair => pair.1 = p)) := by
    have h₇₁ : primes.all (fun p => (findExponents n primes h_precond).any (fun pair => pair.1 = p)) := by
      -- Prove that every prime in the original list appears in the result
      have h₇₂ : ∀ (n : ℕ) (primes : List ℕ), findExponents_precond n primes → primes.all (fun p => (findExponents n primes ‹_›).any (fun pair => pair.1 = p)) := by
        intro n primes h
        have h₇₃ : n > 0 := h.1
        have h₇₄ : primes.length > 0 := h.2.1
        have h₇₅ : primes.all (fun p => Nat.Prime p) := h.2.2.1
        have h₇₆ : List.Nodup primes := h.2.2.2
        -- Use the fact that the countFactors function includes all primes from the original list
        have h₇₇ : primes.all (fun p => (countFactors n primes).any (fun pair => pair.1 = p)) := by
          have h₇₈ : ∀ (n : ℕ) (primes : List ℕ), primes.all (fun p => Nat.Prime p) → primes.all (fun p => (countFactors n primes).any (fun pair => pair.1 = p)) := by
            intro n primes h₇₉
            induction' primes with p ps ih
            · simp [countFactors]
            · simp_all [countFactors, List.all_cons, List.any_cons]
              <;>
              (try {
                have h₈₀ := Nat.Prime.one_lt (by aesop : Nat.Prime p)
                have h₈₁ : p > 1 := by linarith
                aesop
              }) <;>
              (try {
                aesop
              })
          exact h₇₈ n primes h₇₅
        -- Since countFactors is the same as findExponents, we can use the above result
        have h₇₉ : countFactors n primes = findExponents n primes ‹_› := by rfl
        rw [h₇₉] at h₇₇
        exact h₇₇
      -- Apply the general result to our specific case
      have h₈₀ : primes.all (fun p => (findExponents n primes h_precond).any (fun pair => pair.1 = p)) := by
        apply h₇₂
        <;> assumption
      exact h₈₀
    exact h₇₁
  -- Combine all the results to prove the postcondition
  refine' ⟨h₅, h₆, h₇⟩

Suppose `primes = [p₁, p₂, ..., p_k]`.
   - For `n`, compute `(count₁, n₁) = countFactor n p₁ 0`.
     - Then `n = p₁^count₁ * n₁` and `n₁ % p₁ ≠ 0`.
   - For `n₁`, compute `(count₂, n₂) = countFactor n₁ p₂ 0`.
     - Then `n₁ = p₂^count₂ * n₂` and `n₂ % p₂ ≠ 0`.
   - Continue until `(count_k, n_k)`.
   -

-- For example, if `n = 12` and `primes = [2, 3]`, the `findExponents` function returns `[(2, 0), (3, 0)]`, which is incorrect.
    -- Therefore, the theorem statement is not valid with the current implementation of `findExponents`.
    sorry
  exact h₅
